titleStmt.title: Letter from James Davidson to his sister, Kathleen, 7 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Davidson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from James Davidson (1877-1916) to his sister, Kathleen. Davidson thanks his sister Kathleen for food parcels. He writes her that the usual intermittent strafing with little damage is continuing; the weather is cold with snow; the trenches are collapsing and his men spend all their time repairing the encampment. He has a heavy cold although the wound (sustained on 27 Feb) has healed completely. He informs Kathleen that the plane that dropped the bomb that injured him was around again and was nearly shot down. He again refers to the unnecessary anxiety caused by the telegram about his injury and regrets that leave has not yet been reinstated.This letter is part of a large correspondence from Captain James Davidson to his parents. Davidson, who was Managing Director of the Sirocco Engineering Works in Belfast, served with 13 Royal Irish Rifles and the Machine Gun Corps. His letters describe his experiences while training in England and serving in France. He was killed on 1 July 1916, the first day of the Battle of the Somme.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
7/3/16 My dearest Kathleen â I have received two
letters each from you & Mother since I wrote
last & beside the usual parcels there arrived
safely one with mufflers & a box of apples.
My best thanks for all. There is not much
in the way of news the usual intermittent
straffing has been going on with very little damage
at least as far as we are concerned. The
weather however has been perfectly awful â
snow & storm then thaw &more snow.
Today it has almost disappeared but tonight
it has started again & the cold is intense.
The trenches are appalling in some places.
No sooner is one place propped up than
another falls in. My men spend all their
time repairing the emplacements & the approaches.
I have had another bad cold & spent most of
yest in bed but today rather better& able to
be about. Expect I shall be all right again
in a few days but as you can imagine it's
not easy to thrown off a cold under the conditions
out here. The cut in my arm is of course
almost healed up & never gave me a moments
trouble. The same old aeroplane was
round here yest & dropped a couple more
bombs some distance away but he nearly
paid for doing so as one of the shells from our
anti aircraft guns burst just beside him &
made him wobble but evidently the plane was
not seriously hit & he was able to get back
over the German lines. Am so sorry you
all had so much unnecessary anxiety about me.
It really is ridiculous to report such trivial
wounds & I was just afraid they would send you
that wire & naturally make you imagine that it
was much more serious than it really was.
However I dare say my letter telling you all
about it arrived soon after & that would
relieve your minds. There is still no
news of leave re-starting. Hope it will come
soon. Good night dear & my best love
to all. Your loving brother Jim
On Active Service Glenbank Mrs Hadow Seacourt Bangor Co Down Ireland 15 March 1916
J Davidson
7/3/16
